entries.title: the day
entries.content:
Morning sunlight slipped through my curtains before my alarm even had the chance to scream at me. For once, I didn’t immediately grab my phone. I just stayed there for a minute, half awake, listening to the fan spinning above me and the distant sound of someone making tea downstairs. The air felt weirdly peaceful today, like the day had already decided it was going to be kind to me.

I wore my oversized blue hoodie even though it wasn’t cold at all, tied my hair up badly, and walked to the kitchen looking like a sleep-deprived raccoon. Amma laughed at me immediately. She handed me coffee without saying anything because she already knew I was functioning at 2% battery.

After breakfast, I sat near the window with my laptop pretending to “be productive,” but honestly I spent more time staring outside than actually working. The sky looked unreal today. Soft clouds, bright light, tiny breeze moving the trees every few seconds. One of those days where everything feels cinematic for no reason.

Around noon, I went out with my friends. The auto ride itself felt fun because we were laughing over the dumbest things imaginable. At one point we were arguing about whether penguins have knees. Fully serious debate. Full public embarrassment.

We ended up at this tiny café with warm yellow lights and ridiculously overpriced fries that somehow tasted worth it anyway. I don’t know why simple moments hit so hard sometimes. Sitting there, hearing overlapping conversations, ice clinking in glasses, someone’s playlist in the background, my friends bullying me every three minutes — it just felt nice. No pressure. No overthinking. Just existing.

I caught myself smiling randomly a few times today.

After that we walked around without any actual plan. Took blurry pictures. Made fun of each other’s poses. Bought random snacks from a roadside shop. There was this tiny sunset moment while crossing the road where the whole sky turned orange-pink for like five minutes, and everyone suddenly went quiet looking at it. I wanted to freeze that exact feeling somewhere.

By evening I was exhausted in the best way possible. Came home with messy hair, tired legs, and that strange happy heaviness after a long day outside. I showered, changed into pajamas, and sat on my bed scrolling through the photos we took. Half of them are terrible. Most are blurry. But they feel real.

I think that’s what made today special.

Nothing huge happened. No life-changing moment. No dramatic breakthrough. But for one entire day, I wasn’t trapped inside my head worrying about the future or replaying old things. I just lived.

And honestly?
That felt enough.

entries.title: human things
entries.content:
I woke up feeling strangely emotional today, though nothing bad had even happened. Maybe it was one of those mornings where your heart wakes up before your brain does. The room felt quiet in a heavy way, and for a few minutes I just stayed in bed staring at the ceiling, thinking about how fast everything is moving lately.

Still, I forced myself up. Opened the curtains. The weather was cloudy and gray, but not depressing gray. Soft gray. Comforting gray. The kind that makes you want to slow down a little.

I spent most of the morning alone. Made myself coffee, played music through my speaker, and cleaned my room while singing dramatically into a hairbrush like I was performing for a stadium. There’s something healing about cleaning your space when your mind feels messy too. Every folded shirt and cleared table corner felt like I was fixing tiny pieces of myself.

In the afternoon, I met one of my closest friends after weeks of saying “we should meet soon” and never actually doing it. The second I saw her, it felt familiar in the safest way possible. Some people just make life quieter. Easier.

We walked around for hours with absolutely nowhere to be. Talked about relationships, family pressure, college stress, random childhood memories, and those stupid late-night fears we never admit out loud normally. At one point we sat on a low wall near the road eating ice cream while traffic passed by endlessly, and somehow that conversation felt more meaningful than anything I’ve done online in months.

There was this weird moment later where everyone around me looked busy rushing somewhere, and suddenly I felt very small but also very alive at the same time. Like life is huge and confusing and temporary, but maybe that’s exactly why these tiny moments matter so much.

By evening, my mood shifted again. I don’t know why. I started missing people. Missing old versions of myself too. I looked through old photos for a while and got hit with that bittersweet feeling where you’re grateful something happened but sad it’s already over.

It’s strange how a single day can hold so many emotions at once.

Happiness.
Loneliness.
Comfort.
Nostalgia.
Hope.

Tonight I’m sitting near my window with my lamp on, typing this while the rest of the house sleeps. The world outside is quiet except for a few distant vehicle sounds and dogs barking occasionally. I feel calmer now.

Not fully okay.
Not sad either.

Just human, I guess.
